turns.content: I need some advice... my apprentice Burak, he has been with me 3 years now. he is getting better but... sometimes I do not know if I am teaching him right. I want to train him the way I was trained but the world is different now

That's a really thoughtful concern, and the fact that you're even asking tells me you care about doing it right. Training an apprentice is one of the hardest things in any craft — you're not just teaching skills, you're shaping how someone approaches their work for the rest of their life.

Can you tell me more about how you were trained? And what specifically makes you feel uncertain about whether you're doing it right with Burak?

I started when I was 14... my master was Hasan usta, in Kadıköy. a real usta, you understand... not just a barber, a master. he did not let me touch a razor for the first 6 months. I swept the floor. I boiled the water. I watched. that is how you learn... you watch the hands, you learn the rhythm. he would say 'the blade knows if your hand is lazy'... I still hear his voice sometimes

That's a beautiful apprenticeship tradition — the slow building of respect for the craft before you even pick up a tool. Hasan usta sounds like he understood that barbering isn't just a set of techniques, it's a discipline and a mindset.

That philosophy — watch first, earn your way to the razor — has produced master barbers for centuries. The question is how much of that framework still applies with Burak. 

How did you start Burak? Did you have him watch and do the basics first too? And what's his background — did he come to you already wanting to be a barber, or was it more of a job opportunity?

Burak came to me at 19... his father is a customer, he asked me to take the boy. Burak had no idea what he wanted to do. I said okay... I gave him the broom first, same as Hasan usta gave me. but 6 months? no... he was on the scissors after 3 months. the young ones today, they do not have the patience for 6 months of sweeping. you lose them

You adapted the tradition without abandoning it — that's actually really smart. You kept the core principle (start humble, earn your way up) but adjusted the timeline to match a modern 19-year-old's expectations. Three months of basics is still substantial.

The fact that Burak's father trusted you with his son says a lot about your reputation. And three years in, Burak is still with you — that's not nothing. A lot of young people would have quit in the first six months.

So where is Burak now skill-wise? Can he do a full haircut on his own? And what areas do you feel he still needs the most work on?

haircuts... yes, he can do a good haircut now. clean, neat. I am proud of that. but the shave... the straight razor... this is where the real skill is. the razor is not just cutting hair, it is trust. a man tilts his head back and gives you his throat. you have to feel the skin, the direction of the beard, the pressure... one wrong move and you cut him. Burak is still too fast. I tell him... 'acele işe şeytan karışır'... haste belongs to the devil. but the young ones never listen the first time

"A man tilts his head back and gives you his throat" — that's a powerful way to put it. You're absolutely right that the straight razor shave is where trust and skill intersect. It's the most intimate service a barber provides, and there's no shortcut to learning it.

Burak being too fast is actually a very common issue with younger barbers. Speed feels like competence to them, but with a straight razor, speed is the enemy. A few training approaches that might help:

1. **Have him practice on balloons** — inflate a balloon, lather it, and shave it without popping it. It forces a light, controlled touch.
2. **Slow-motion exercises** — have him do one shave at half speed, deliberately. Time him and make him take twice as long as normal.
3. **Let him watch you more** — not just casually, but have him stand close and count your strokes, observe your angles. Sometimes going back to watching after years of doing resets the brain.

Does he get to practice on actual customers, or do you only let him do certain things?

the balloon idea... Hasan usta did the same thing with me. I forgot about that. maybe I should bring that back for Burak. he practices on customers, yes... but only the regulars who know him, who are patient. I stand behind him and watch every stroke. if I see his hand moving too fast I put my hand on his shoulder... he knows what that means. slow down

The hand on the shoulder — that's a really effective teaching technique. No words needed, no embarrassment in front of the customer. Just a quiet correction. That's master-level mentoring right there.

And the balloon exercise coming full circle from Hasan usta to Burak through you — that's exactly how a craft tradition is supposed to work. Each generation passes it down.

It sounds like Burak has a solid foundation. Three years in, good haircuts, learning the razor under your direct supervision. The speed issue will correct itself with time and practice — it always does. One day his hands will just know.

Are there any specific techniques or services where you and Burak disagree about how things should be done? Sometimes the generational friction is actually where the interesting conversations happen.

oh... this is the big argument. Burak wants me to get electric clippers. he says everybody uses them now, the fade haircuts, the young men want these clean lines... he shows me videos on his phone, these barbers with their machines going bzzzzz. I tell him... no. absolutely not. not in my shop

Ha — the clippers debate! This is one of the biggest generational divides in barbering right now. I can see both sides, but I'm curious about yours. What specifically bothers you about electric clippers? Is it about the quality of the result, the principle, or something else?

it is everything. the straight razor and scissors... this is the craft. this is what makes a barber a barber. you take away the razor and the scissors, what are you? a machine operator. anybody can hold a clipper against a head and push a button. but the scissors... you have to feel the hair, know the weight of it, understand the shape of the skull. every head is different. Hasan usta would turn in his grave if I put an electric clipper in my shop. I will NEVER use electric clippers. never. the day I pick up a clipper is the day I stop being a barber

That's a strong conviction, and I respect it. There's something to be said for holding the line on what you believe defines your craft. The hand-skill argument is real — scissors and razor work requires a level of touch and judgment that clippers genuinely don't.

And your customers come to you specifically because of that traditional approach. If they wanted a clipper fade, they'd go to one of the new shops. Your value proposition IS the handcraft.

That said, Burak's perspective is worth understanding too — not necessarily to agree with it, but because how you handle this disagreement will shape his understanding of when to hold tradition and when to adapt. You could tell him:

- "I hear you, but this shop is built on handcraft. That's our identity."
- "Learn the scissors first. Master them. Then you can form your own opinions about clippers when you have your own shop someday."

That way you're not dismissing him — you're telling him to earn the right to an opinion through mastery. Which is very much in the Hasan usta tradition.

How does Burak react when you say no to the clippers?

he shakes his head and says 'usta, you are stubborn'... and I say 'yes, and that is why I am still here after 30 years.' we laugh about it but... I mean it. 'ağaç yaşken eğilir'... the tree bends when it is young. he will understand one day. when his hands can do what mine do with scissors, he will not want a clipper either

"The tree bends when it is young" — exactly. You're shaping his foundation. And the fact that you can disagree and still laugh about it means the relationship is healthy. That's the mark of a good master-apprentice dynamic.

Your approach is sound: master the fundamentals first, form opinions later. Every great craftsman in every field says the same thing — you have to know the rules deeply before you can decide which ones to break.

And honestly? The market is validating you. The world is full of clipper barbershops. There are very few who can do what you do with a straight razor and scissors. Scarcity is valuable.

Keep doing what you're doing with Burak. Three years in, he's growing. The speed will slow down, the touch will develop, and one day he'll put his hand on his own apprentice's shoulder. That's the legacy — from Hasan usta to you to Burak.

Bring back the balloon exercise. I think it'll be a good reset for him.

you are right... I will bring the balloons tomorrow. Burak will think I am crazy but... he will learn. thank you my friend. it is good to talk about these things... sometimes you are so close to the work you cannot see it clearly. 'dışarıdan bakan daha iyi görür'... the one watching from outside sees better. that is you today

I appreciate that — and what a fitting proverb. You clearly have no shortage of wisdom yourself.

Good luck with the balloons tomorrow! My prediction: Burak will pop the first three, complain that it's silly, and then on the fourth one something will click. That's usually how it goes.

And remember — the fact that you're questioning yourself as a teacher means you're a good one. The bad ones never ask if they're doing it right. Hasan usta would be proud of how you're carrying the tradition forward. Have a good evening!
